1:
"And it came to pass, that, as he was praying in a certain place, when he ceased, one of his disciples said unto him, Lord, teach us to pray, as John also taught his disciples." The witnesses note Christ's example of prayer and the disciples' request. The fathers explain why the sinless Christ prayed. Cyril: "whereas He possesses every good in abundance, why does He pray... To this we answer, that it befits Him, according to the manner of His dispensation in the flesh, to follow human observances... that He might teach us not to be lukewarm in this duty, but more diligent and earnest in our prayers." Titus Bostrensis: "the disciples, having seen a new way of life, desire a new form of prayer."

The puritan Henry marks Luke's special notice of Christ at prayer: "This evangelist has taken particular notice of Christ's praying often, more than any other." On the request: "Lord, teach us to pray, is itself a good prayer, and a very needful one, for it is a hard thing to pray well, and it is Jesus Christ only that can teach us." On the plea "as John taught his disciples," Lightfoot (and Henry) note John taught prayers "more filled up with petitions and requests," whereas the Jews' prayers "were generally adorations and doxologies."

The grammatical voices note the relation to Matthew's version. Ellicott: "The reproduction, with only a verbal variation here and there... of what had been given in the Sermon on the Mount, is every way significant." The differences between the two forms (Luke shorter, omitting petitions and the doxology) the witnesses read as showing the prayer was given as a directory, not a rigid form to which we are tied — though Wesley holds we are "directed not only to imitate this but to use this very form."

The witnesses agree: as Christ prayed (Luke's special emphasis), a disciple asked to be taught to pray, as John had taught his disciples — itself a needful prayer, for only Christ can teach us to pray well (Henry). The sinless Christ prayed, the fathers note, not from need but to teach us diligence by his example (Cyril). The disciples sought a fuller, more petitionary form than the Jewish doxologies (Lightfoot). The verse opens the giving of the Lord's Prayer, whose shorter Lukan form (against Matthew's) shows it to be a directory and model as well as a form of words. Christ, the great Teacher of prayer, answers the request that all believers should make — that he would teach them, by his word and Spirit, how to pray; and the prayer he gives becomes the pattern and substance of all Christian prayer.
2:
"And he said unto them, When ye pray, say, Our Father which art in heaven, Hallowed be thy name. Thy kingdom come. Thy will be done, as in heaven, so in earth." The witnesses expound the opening address and the first petitions. On "Our Father," the fathers dwell on the grace of adoption. Pseudo-Augustine: "The first word, how gracious is it!... From an evil servant you are made a good son... Of Christ alone is He the especial Father, of us the common Father. For Christ alone He begot, but us he created." Cyril of Jerusalem: "On them who revolted from Him... has He bestowed such a complete forgiveness... as that they should even call Him Father." Theophylact: "which art in heaven" is said "not as though He were confined to that place, but to raise the hearer up to heaven."

On "Hallowed be thy name," the fathers read it as both God's name being glorified in our lives and being made known to the world. Gregory of Nyssa: "May I through Your concurring aid be made just, abstaining from all evil." Origen: "that the name of God may be appropriated to the only true God." Pseudo-Augustine: "let Your holiness be known to all the world."

On "Thy kingdom come," Origen reads it as the putting down of all rival rule and sin; Gregory of Nyssa (per some) as "May Your Holy Spirit come upon us to purify us." Cyril takes it of the awful final coming. Augustine notes Luke omits the third petition ("Thy will be done"), which Matthew has, "showing that the third petition is in a manner a repetition of the two former" — though the witnesses note the better texts of Luke do include it.

The witnesses agree: Christ teaches us to address God as "Our Father" — the grace of adoption, making sons of revolted servants (Pseudo-Augustine, Cyril of Jerusalem) — and to begin with petitions for God's glory before our own needs. "Hallowed be thy name" prays that God's name be sanctified in our lives and known to all the world; "Thy kingdom come," that his reign put down all sin and rival rule; "Thy will be done," that earth conform to heaven's obedience. The verse displays the order and substance of true prayer: the soul, raised to heaven and made a child by grace, seeks first the hallowing of God's name, the coming of his kingdom, and the doing of his will, before turning to its own wants. To call God "Father" is the gift of grace; to seek his glory first is the order of faith.
3:
"Give us day by day our daily bread." The witnesses note the petition for daily provision and debate whether the "bread" is literal or spiritual. The phrase "daily" (Greek epiousios) is variously understood. Pseudo-Augustine: "In the Greek the word is epiousios, that is, something added to the substance. It is not that bread which goes into the body, but that bread of everlasting life, which supports the substance of our soul" — he reads it of the Eucharist, "the heavenly and dreadful Sacrament." Titus Bostrensis takes it of "the Divine power, bringing the everlasting life."

Against the wholly spiritual reading, Cyril and Chrysostom hold the literal sense, with a lesson of contentment. Cyril: "from the fact that He bids them ask for bread, that is daily food, it seems that He implies that they should possess nothing, but rather practise an honourable poverty. For it is not the part of those who have bread to seek it, but of those who are oppressed with want." Chrysostom: "not varieties of meats... but bread which is able to support the bodily substance, sufficient only for the day, that we may take no thought of the morrow."

The petition the witnesses read as teaching dependence on God for our needs. Basil: "For your daily bread, trust not to yourself, but fly to God for it, making known to Him the necessities of your nature." Gill notes "day by day" or "for the day." Whether literal or spiritual, the petition casts the believer wholly on God's daily provision.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree that we are taught to ask God for our "daily bread," but divide on its meaning: the literal bread of daily need, teaching contentment and honest poverty (Cyril, Chrysostom, Basil), or the spiritual bread of everlasting life — the word, the Eucharist, the "supersubstantial" food of the soul (Pseudo-Augustine, Titus, per the Greek epiousios). Either way, the petition teaches dependence on God for our daily provision, body and soul. The verse displays the humility of true prayer: having sought God's glory (v.2), the believer asks only for the day's necessities, trusting the Father for what each day requires, and taking no anxious thought for the morrow. To ask for daily bread is to confess our dependence and to renounce both anxious hoarding and self-sufficiency; the Father who feeds us day by day is to be trusted, not doubted, for body and soul alike.
4:
"And forgive us our sins; for we also forgive every one that is indebted to us. And lead us not into temptation; but deliver us from evil." The witnesses note the petitions for forgiveness and against temptation. On forgiveness, the fathers stress the reciprocal condition. Cyril of Alexandria: "He first commands them to ask forgiveness of the sins they commit, and then to confess that they also entirely forgive others... that same gentleness which they show to their fellow servants, they pray that they may receive in equal measure from God." Cyril: "He wishes to make God the imitator of the patience which men practise." Barnes guards it: "unless we forgive others, God will not forgive us... not that by forgiving others we deserve forgiveness... but that this is a disposition without which God cannot consistently pardon us." Wesley: "This does not denote the meritorious cause of our pardon, but the removal of that hindrance."

On "lead us not into temptation," the witnesses note the meaning. Cyril of Jerusalem: "the entering into temptation means the being overwhelmed by the temptation... Those not overwhelmed pass through, showing themselves excellent swimmers." Titus Bostrensis: "it is impossible not to be tempted by the devil, but we make this prayer that we may not be abandoned to our temptations."

On "deliver us from evil," which Luke's better texts omit, the witnesses note its meaning and place. Lightfoot and Clarke: "Deliver us from evil is an inadequate rendering of rhusai hemas apo tou ponerou; literally, Deliver us from the wicked one" — Lightfoot reads it especially against the devil's apparitions. Augustine: Luke omits it "that we might understand it belongs to the former" petition about temptation — "Do not this, but this," one petition.

The witnesses agree: Christ teaches us to ask forgiveness of sins on the reciprocal condition that we forgive others — not as the meritorious cause of pardon but as the disposition without which God cannot consistently pardon (Barnes, Wesley) — and to pray not to be overwhelmed by temptation but delivered from "the wicked one" (Clarke, Lightfoot). The "deliver us from evil," omitted in Luke's text, belongs (Augustine) to the temptation petition. The verse displays the humility and dependence of true prayer: the believer, having sought God's glory and his daily bread, confesses his sins, forgives his debtors, and casts himself on God for protection against temptation and the evil one. To pray for forgiveness while forgiving, and for deliverance from the tempter, is to acknowledge both our guilt and our weakness, and to rest wholly on the mercy and keeping of the Father.
5:
"And he said unto them, Which of you shall have a friend, and shall go unto him at midnight, and say unto him, Friend, lend me three loaves." The witnesses note the parable of the friend at midnight, teaching importunity in prayer. Cyril introduces it: "it might happen that those who had received this wholesome teaching poured forth their prayers... carelessly and languidly, and then when not heard in the first or second prayer, left off praying. That this might not be our case, he shows by a parable that cowardice in prayer is hurtful, but patience is of great advantage."

The grammatical voices note the vividness and the lesson. Ellicott: "The illustration is peculiar to St. Luke, and, as setting forth the power of prayer, is specially characteristic of him... Midnight is chosen as being the time at which men expect to be left to their repose. The unexpected visitor asks for three loaves." Barnes: "His design is solely to show the necessity of being importunate or persevering in prayer to God." Calvin: "Believers ought not to be discouraged, if they do not immediately obtain their desires... if, among men, importunity extorts what a person would not willingly do, we have no reason to doubt that God will listen to us, if we persevere."

The fathers read an allegory. Augustine: the three loaves "the food of the heavenly mystery"; the friend on a journey "in this present life, in which all are travelling as strangers"; the one knocking at midnight in the famine of the word, when the teachers "are now in their secret rest with the Lord." Ambrose: prayer should be offered "not only in the day, but at night," as David at midnight gave thanks.

The witnesses agree: the parable of the friend at midnight teaches importunity and perseverance in prayer — lest, discouraged by delay, we leave off praying (Cyril). The vivid picture (peculiar to Luke, Ellicott) of one asking bread at the most inconvenient hour sets forth the power of persevering prayer: if human importunity extorts a favour, how much more will God answer those who persevere (Calvin). The fathers add an allegory of the three loaves (the heavenly mystery, the Trinity), the friend on a journey (this transient life), and the midnight (the famine of the word). The verse displays the encouragement to importunate prayer: the believer is to come boldly, even at the most unseasonable time, and to keep asking; for delay is not denial, and God, far kinder than the reluctant friend, will surely answer persevering faith. Wesley: "no time is unseasonable with God."
6:
"For a friend of mine in his journey is come to me, and I have nothing to set before him." The brief verse continues the parable, giving the reason for the midnight request. Clarke clarifies the urgency: "A friend of mine is come to me out of his way, which renders the case more urgent — a friend of mine, benighted, belated, and who has lost his way, is come unto me. This was a strong reason why he should have prompt relief." Robertson notes "to set before him" (paratheso), the same verb used at the feeding of the five thousand (9:16).

The friend's coming "in his journey" the witnesses (with Augustine, under v.5) read allegorically of this present life, in which all are travellers and strangers, and the host's having "nothing to set before him" as the lack the believer feels when asked for spiritual food he cannot supply, driving him to seek it from God.

The host's empty cupboard the witnesses note as the occasion of the importunate request. Having nothing to give his unexpected guest, the man is compelled to seek bread from his friend at midnight — a picture of the believer who, conscious of his own emptiness, is driven to ask of God what he lacks. The need supplies the motive for the persevering prayer.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the host's reason for the midnight request is the arrival of a benighted, travelling friend whom he has nothing to feed (Clarke) — a picture, in the patristic allegory (Augustine, under v.5), of this present life in which all are travellers, and of the believer driven by his own emptiness to seek from God the food he cannot himself supply. The verse displays the motive of importunate prayer: conscious need. The man asks at midnight not for himself only but for another, and because he has nothing — and so the believer, feeling his own lack and the needs of those he would serve, is driven to ask of God. The empty cupboard becomes the occasion of persevering prayer; and the consciousness of need, far from discouraging, presses the soul to keep knocking at the door of heaven. Those who feel their emptiness most are most driven to ask; and God, who alone can supply, is the friend to whom such need must turn.
7:
"And he from within shall answer and say, Trouble me not: the door is now shut, and my children are with me in bed; I cannot rise and give thee." The witnesses note the reluctant friend's refusal and its place in the argument. Ellicott reads it as an à fortiori: "the illustrative matter cannot be pressed into an interpretation... Thus man might answer, but so does not God. If prayer prevails over apathy and impatience, how much more will it prevail when we pray to One who knows our necessities before we ask Him?" The picture is of a poor man's house, "children and parents sleeping in the same room."

The grammatical voices note the realism. Robertson: "The door is now shut (perfect passive, shut to stay shut). Oriental locks are not easy to unlock... Often a whole family would sleep in the same room. I cannot — that is, I am not willing." Clarke notes the textual addition in some versions ("but if he persevere knocking"), "necessarily implied, for unless the man be represented as continuing to solicit, he could not be said to use importunity."

The refusal the witnesses read as the foil to God's readiness. The reluctant friend, unwilling to rise for friendship's sake, yields only to persistence; God, by contrast, knows our needs, loves to give, and is no reluctant sleeper. The à fortiori argument turns on the contrast: if even a grudging man yields to importunity, how much more the willing Father.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the reluctant friend refuses at first — the door shut, the family in bed, unwilling to rise — and yields only to persistence (the "I cannot" meaning "I am not willing," Robertson). The refusal is the foil to God's readiness (Ellicott): if even a grudging man yields to importunity, how much more the Father who knows our needs and loves to give. The illustration cannot be pressed into an allegory of God as reluctant; the argument is à fortiori, from the lesser to the greater. The verse displays the contrast between the reluctant human friend and the willing God: human importunity overcomes apathy and inconvenience, but God needs no such overcoming — he is ever ready to give, and our perseverance is for our sake, not to wear down an unwilling God. The shut door of the parable only magnifies the open door of heaven; and the grudging neighbour's eventual yielding assures us that the gracious Father will far more readily answer the prayer of his children. Persevere, for God is no reluctant sleeper but a willing Giver.
8:
"I say unto you, Though he will not rise and give him, because he is his friend, yet because of his importunity he will rise and give him as many as he needeth." The witnesses note the point of the parable — importunity prevails. The grammatical voices explain "importunity." Ellicott: "Literally, because of his shamelessness. The word is not used elsewhere in the New Testament, and exactly expresses the pertinacity that knows no restraint." Robertson: "from anaides, shameless... shamelessness, impudence. Examples of importunate prayer Vincent notes Abraham in behalf of Sodom (Genesis 18) and the Syro-Phoenician woman (Matthew 15)."

Barnes draws out the application to prayer: "Rather than be disturbed, he would rise and give what was asked. This is to be applied to God in no other sense than that he often hears prayers and grants blessings even long after they appear to be unanswered or withheld... He tries their faith. He leaves them to persevere for months or years, until they feel entirely their dependence on him... Let us go again and again, until the prayer is heard." The note (in the Vulgate addition) that "if he shall continue knocking" makes the importunity, not a single asking, the point.

The lesson the witnesses read as encouragement to persevering prayer. As the shameless persistence of the man prevailed over the reluctant friend, so persevering prayer prevails with God — not because God is reluctant, but because he often delays to try our faith, deepen our dependence, and prepare us to receive. The believer is to keep asking until the blessing comes.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, the application of the parable. The witnesses agree: the point is that importunity — literally "shamelessness," a pertinacity that knows no restraint (Ellicott, Robertson) — prevails, even where friendship does not move the reluctant man. Applied to prayer (not making God reluctant), this teaches that God often delays the answer to try faith, deepen dependence, and prepare us to receive, so that we must persevere "again and again, until the prayer is heard" (Barnes). The verse displays the power of persevering, even importunate prayer: as Abraham pleaded for Sodom and the Syro-Phoenician woman for her daughter, so the believer is to press his petition without restraint until God answers. The shameless persistence that wins over a grudging neighbour is the very boldness God invites in prayer — not to overcome his reluctance, but to exercise and strengthen our faith. Let the believer, then, take no denial, but keep knocking at heaven's door until it opens.
9:
"And I say unto you, Ask, and it shall be given you; seek, and ye shall find; knock, and it shall be opened unto you." The witnesses note the threefold encouragement to prayer. The fathers distinguish the three. Chrysostom: "by asking, He means prayer; by seeking, zeal and anxiety... For those things which are sought require great care... Knock, and it shall be opened; for if you continue seeking, you shall surely receive." The Greek Exposition: "to ask is to pray, to seek is by good works to do things becoming our prayers, and to knock is to continue in prayer without ceasing." Augustine: "He would not so encourage us to ask were He not willing to give. Let human slothfulness blush: He is more willing to give than we to receive."

Augustine adds a further allegory of the gifts asked (bread, fish, egg): "by the bread is meant charity... by the fish, the belief in invisible things... by the egg is understood hope" — opposed by the stone (hardness), the serpent (deceit), and the scorpion (looking back). Cyril: "The words, I say to you, have the force of an oath. For God does not lie."

The grammatical voices and Origen note the condition. Origen: "whoso sets about seeking in the right way... shall really receive... But if a man turns away from the object of a right petition, and asks not as it becomes him, he does not ask" — citing James, "you ask and receive not, because you ask amiss." Clarke: "Be importunate with God, not so much to prevail on him to save you, as to get yourselves brought into a proper disposition to receive."

The witnesses agree: Christ encourages prayer with a threefold promise — ask, seek, knock — which the fathers distinguish as the rising stages of prayer (petition, diligent zeal, persevering importunity) and the Greek Exposition as prayer, works becoming prayer, and unceasing continuance. The promise has "the force of an oath" (Cyril), for God cannot lie and is "more willing to give than we to receive" (Augustine). Yet the asking must be right: those who ask amiss, for vain ends, do not truly ask (Origen, James). The verse displays the boundless encouragement to prayer: God invites us to ask, seek, and knock, and binds himself to answer. The condition is a right asking — in faith, for good ends, with perseverance. To ask, seek, and knock is to exercise the faith that lays hold of God's willingness; and the threefold promise assures the persevering believer that he shall receive, find, and have the door of heaven opened to him.
10:
"For every one that asketh receiveth; and he that seeketh findeth; and to him that knocketh it shall be opened." The brief verse restates the promise of v.9 as a universal rule. Gill notes the apparent exception and its resolution: "Some indeed ask and receive not, because they ask amiss, who either apply to a wrong person, or ask in a wrong manner, or from wrong principles, or with wrong ends in view: but when a man is right in the object of prayer, and in the matter and manner of prayer, and in the end he proposes... let him be what he will, of whatsoever nation, or ever mean a person, he has the petitions which he asks, either immediately, or at least in God's due time."

The universal "every one" the witnesses read as the breadth of the promise. No suppliant who asks rightly is excluded — whatever his nation, rank, or meanness, the one who asks, seeks, and knocks in faith and for good ends shall receive, find, and have the door opened. The promise is to all who pray aright.

The qualification "in God's due time" the witnesses note as reconciling the promise with the experience of delay. The believer receives either immediately or in God's appointed season; the delay (as the parable of the friend at midnight, vv.5-8, taught) is not denial but the trial and strengthening of faith. The promise is sure, though its timing is God's.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse of this section. The witnesses agree: the promise of v.9 is restated as a universal rule — "every one that asketh receiveth" — embracing all who ask rightly, whatever their nation, rank, or meanness (Gill). The apparent exceptions (those who ask and receive not) are resolved: they ask amiss, for wrong ends or in wrong manner; but the one who asks in faith, for good ends, with right object and manner, shall surely receive, either at once or in God's due time. The verse displays the breadth and certainty of the promise to prayer: it excludes no suppliant who comes aright, and assures every such one of an answer. The delay that faith sometimes meets is not denial but the appointed season of God, who answers in his own best time. Let the believer, then, ask in confidence, knowing that the promise is universal and sure: every one who asks rightly receives, every seeker finds, and to every knocker the door is opened. The God who invites prayer does not mock the asker but answers him.
11:
"If a son shall ask bread of any of you that is a father, will he give him a stone? or if he ask a fish, will he for a fish give him a serpent?" The witnesses note the argument from human fatherhood to the heavenly Father's goodness. Cyril of Alexandria draws the lesson: "oftentimes we rashly, from the impulse of pleasure, give way to hurtful desires. When we ask any such thing from God, we shall not obtain it... For when your son asks of you bread, you give it him gladly, because he seeks a wholesome food. But when from want of understanding he asks for a stone to eat, you give it him not, but rather hinders him."

The grammatical voices note the difficult construction. Robertson: "There is a decided anacoluthon here... It is a very awkward piece of Greek and yet it is intelligible." The argument compares the loaf with the stone (similar in appearance) and the fish with the serpent (the eel-like serpent), the point being that no good father gives his child a harmful thing in place of the wholesome thing asked.

The argument the witnesses read as the ground of confidence in prayer. If earthly fathers, though evil, give good gifts to their children, how much more will the heavenly Father (v.13)? And the comparison also reassures the believer that God, like a wise father, will not grant a harmful request even when we ask amiss, but gives what is truly good. The Father's goodness both answers right prayer and withholds what would hurt us.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ argues from human fatherhood to God's goodness — no good father, asked for bread or fish, gives his child a stone or a serpent (harmful things resembling the good). The argument grounds confidence in prayer: if earthly fathers give good gifts, how much more the heavenly Father (v.13). Cyril adds the comfort that God, like a wise father, withholds even what we ask amiss, when it would harm us, giving only what is truly good. The verse displays the fatherly goodness of God as the ground of prayer's confidence: the believer may ask boldly, assured that the Father will give what is good and withhold what is harmful, even when our own requests are mistaken. The God to whom we pray is no grudging neighbour (v.7) but a loving Father, who delights to give his children good gifts and will never answer their need with a stone or a serpent. Such a Father may be trusted utterly in prayer.
12:
"Or if he shall ask an egg, will he offer him a scorpion?" The brief verse completes the threefold comparison (bread/stone, fish/serpent, egg/scorpion). The grammatical voices note the resemblance that gives the comparison force. Barnes cites Dr. Thomson: "old writers speak of a white scorpion, and such a one, with the tail folded up... would not look unlike a small egg." Clarke and Gill discuss the natural history — a scorpion (or a serpent's egg) given for a nourishing egg would be deadly.

The point the witnesses read as the same as v.11: no good father gives his child a harmful thing in place of the wholesome thing asked. The egg (nourishing) and the scorpion (venomous, resembling a folded white scorpion an egg) complete the picture of the contrast between the good gift sought and the harmful thing a wicked father might give.

The argument the witnesses (with Cyril, under v.11) read as grounding confidence in the heavenly Father's goodness. If even evil earthly fathers do not mock their children with harmful gifts, how much more will the good God give good things to those who ask him (v.13). The comparison reassures the believer that God answers prayer with what is truly good, never with what would harm.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse of this section. The witnesses agree: the third comparison (egg/scorpion) completes the picture — no good father gives his child a venomous scorpion (resembling a folded white egg, Barnes) in place of the nourishing egg he asks. The point, as in v.11, is the fatherly goodness that gives good gifts and withholds harmful ones. The argument grounds the believer's confidence in prayer: if earthly fathers, though evil, do not mock their children with deadly gifts, how much more will the heavenly Father give good things (v.13) to those who ask. The verse displays, by a vivid natural image, the goodness of God in answering prayer: he gives the nourishing, not the venomous; the wholesome, not the harmful. The believer may therefore ask in full confidence, knowing that the Father will never answer his child's need with a scorpion, but with what is truly good. The God of prayer is a Father whose gifts are always for our good; and the three homely comparisons (stone, serpent, scorpion) assure us that he will never betray the trust of those who ask him.
13:
"If ye then, being evil, know how to give good gifts unto your children: how much more shall your heavenly Father give the Holy Spirit to them that ask him?" The witnesses note the climactic argument and Luke's distinctive "Holy Spirit." The fathers (under v.9) draw out the comparison. Bede: "The Apostles even... are said to be evil in comparison with Divine goodness, since nothing is of itself good but God alone." On the gift, Bede: "the Holy Spirit is the fullness of God's gifts, since all the advantages received from the grace of God's gifts flow from that source." Athanasius: "unless the Holy Spirit were of the substance of God, who alone is good, He would by no means be called good."

The grammatical voices note Luke's substitution. Ellicott: "the one highest gift of the Holy Spirit taking the place of the wider and less definite good things in Matthew. The variation is significant, as belonging to a later stage of our Lord's teaching, and especially as spoken probably to some of the Seventy, who were thus taught to ask boldly for the Spirit." Robertson: "Only here Jesus adds the Holy Spirit as the great gift that the Father is ready to bestow." Wesley: "How beautiful is the gradation! A friend: a father: God! Give the Holy Spirit — the best of gifts, and that which includes every good gift."

The argument the witnesses read as the crown of the teaching on prayer. From the lesser (evil men giving good gifts) to the greater (the good God giving the best gift), the believer is assured that the Father will give the Holy Spirit — the sum of all blessings — to those who ask. Henry: "we must ask for the Holy Spirit, not only as necessary in order to our praying well, but as all spiritual blessings are included in that one."

The witnesses agree: the climactic argument moves from the lesser to the greater — if evil earthly fathers give good gifts, how much more will the good heavenly Father give the Holy Spirit to those who ask. Luke's "Holy Spirit" (for Matthew's "good things") names the highest gift, the fullness of all God's blessings (Bede, Robertson), perhaps spoken to the Seventy that they might ask boldly for the Spirit (Ellicott). The beautiful gradation — friend, father, God (Wesley) — assures the believer of the Father's surpassing willingness to give. The verse crowns the teaching on prayer: the God who invites us to ask, seek, and knock will give, to those who ask, the best of gifts, the Holy Spirit, in whom all spiritual blessings are included (Henry). To ask for the Spirit is to ask for the sum of all good; and the Father, far better than any earthly parent, delights to give this gift to his children. Let the believer, then, ask boldly for the Holy Spirit, assured of the Father's more-than-willing answer.
14:
"And he was casting out a devil, and it was dumb. And it came to pass, when the devil was gone out, the dumb spake; and the people wondered." The witnesses note the miracle that occasions the Beelzebub controversy. The fathers read the dumb spirit. Titus Bostrensis: "He calls the devil deaf or dumb, as being the cause of this calamity, that the Divine word should not be heard. For the devil, by taking away the quickness of human feeling, blunts the hearing of our soul." Bede notes Matthew adds the man was blind: "Three miracles then were performed at the same time... The blind see, the dumb speaks, and he that was possessed is set free. The like is daily accomplished in the conversion of believers."

The Gloss connects it to the preceding promise: "The Lord had promised that the Holy Spirit should be given to those that asked; the blessed effects whereof He shows in the following miracle." The casting out of the dumb spirit, opening the man's mouth, figures the work of the Spirit who frees the soul to hear and speak God's word.

The grammatical voices note the crowd's wonder and the Pharisees' reaction. The people marvelled; but (as the following verses show) the Pharisees, unable to deny the miracle, perverted it as the work of Beelzebub (v.15), while others demanded a sign from heaven (v.16). The miracle that should have led to faith became the occasion of blasphemy.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which opens the Beelzebub controversy. The witnesses agree: Christ cast out a dumb (and, per Matthew, blind) demon, and the freed man spoke, to the crowd's wonder. The fathers read the dumb spirit as the cause of the soul's deafness to God's word, and the cure as figuring the Spirit's work in conversion — the blind seeing, the dumb speaking the praises of God (Bede). The miracle is the "blessed effect" of the Spirit promised in v.13 (Gloss). The verse displays Christ's power over the demonic and its spiritual significance: the casting out of the spirit that stops the ear and tongue frees the man to hear and speak — as in every conversion the Spirit opens the heart to God's word. Yet the miracle that drew the crowd's wonder would draw the Pharisees' blasphemy (v.15): the same work of power that should have led to faith became, in hardened hearts, the occasion of the most desperate unbelief. The opened mouth of the healed man rebukes the closed hearts of his accusers.
15:
"But some of them said, He casteth out devils through Beelzebub the chief of the devils." The witnesses note the Pharisees' blasphemous explanation of the miracle. The grammatical voices stress its desperation. Robertson: "It was useless to deny the fact of the miracles. So they were explained as wrought by Satan himself, a most absurd explanation." The miracle being undeniable, the Pharisees attributed it to the prince of demons rather than confess the power of God.

On the name "Beelzebub," Lightfoot discusses the form: he prefers "Beelzebul" (with the final l) against the versions reading "Beelzebub." Wesley explains: "Beelzebub signifies the lord of flies, a title the heathens gave to Jupiter... The Philistines worshipped a deity under this name, as the god of Ekron; from hence the Jews took the name, and applied it to the chief of the devils." Bede (under v.14): "Beelzebub was the god of Ekron... called the man of flies, from whose most foul practices the chief of the devils was so named."

The charge the witnesses read as the sin of attributing the work of the Spirit to Satan — the blasphemy against the Holy Spirit. Rather than acknowledge the undeniable miracle as God's, the Pharisees, hardened in envy, ascribed it to the devil — a desperate and absurd explanation that exposed the malice of their unbelief.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the Pharisees, unable to deny the miracle, ascribed it to Beelzebub the chief of demons — "a most absurd explanation" (Robertson) born of their hardened envy. The name (Beelzebul/Beelzebub, the "lord of flies," the god of Ekron, Lightfoot, Wesley) was the Jews' title for the prince of demons. The charge is the blasphemy against the Holy Spirit: attributing the manifest work of God's Spirit to Satan. The verse displays the desperate malice of unbelief: confronted with an undeniable work of divine power, the Pharisees would rather credit it to the devil than confess it as God's. Their explanation, absurd on its face (for how could Satan cast out Satan, v.18), exposed the hardness of hearts that had resolved to oppose the truth at any cost. The opened mouth of the healed man (v.14) is met not with faith but with blasphemy; and the Pharisees, by ascribing the Spirit's work to the demonic, reveal the depth of the unbelief that no miracle could cure.
16:
"And others, tempting him, sought of him a sign from heaven." The witnesses note a second group demanding a sign. The grammatical voices explain the demand. Robertson: "These others apparently realized the futility of the charge of being in league with Beelzebub. Hence they put up to Jesus the demand for a sign from heaven... a great spectacular display of heavenly power such as they expected the Messiah to give." Wesley: "Others, to try whether it were so or no, sought a sign from heaven."

The Reformed Calvin reads the demand as a pretext for unbelief: "They ask a sign, in order to plead, as a plausible pretence for their unbelief, that Christ's calling has not been duly attested... He had already performed miracles before their eyes sufficiently numerous and manifest; but as if these were not enough, they wish to have something exhibited from heaven... they apologize for not believing the Gospel on this pretence, that Christ shows no sign from heaven." Bede (under v.14): they sought a sign like Moses parting the sea or Joshua staying the sun.

The demand the witnesses read as the wickedness of unbelief that will not be satisfied. Having seen abundant miracles, these tempters demanded a heavenly portent — not from a willingness to believe, but to excuse their unbelief. The demand for a sign, like the Beelzebub charge, was a refusal to acknowledge the evidence already given.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: a second group, "tempting" Christ, demanded a sign from heaven — a spectacular heavenly portent such as they expected of the Messiah (Robertson). Calvin reads it as a pretext for unbelief: having seen abundant miracles, they demanded a heavenly sign not to believe but to excuse their not believing, alleging that Christ's calling was not duly attested. The verse displays the wickedness of an unbelief that no evidence can satisfy: where the Pharisees blasphemed the miracle (v.15), these tempters dismissed it and demanded more, seeking a sign from heaven as a plausible cover for their refusal to believe the signs already given. The demand for a sign, like the Beelzebub charge, was at bottom a hardened resolve to reject Christ — for those who will not believe the works before their eyes would not believe though a sign came from heaven itself. Unbelief, when determined, always finds a pretext; and the demand for more evidence is often only the excuse of a heart that has already refused.
17:
"But he, knowing their thoughts, said unto them, Every kingdom divided against itself is brought to desolation; and a house divided against a house falleth." The witnesses note Christ's reply to the Beelzebub charge — the argument from a divided kingdom. Chrysostom: "He did not answer them from the Scriptures, since they explained them away falsely; but he answers from things of every day occurrence. For a house and a city if divided is quickly scattered... If then I cast out devils by means of a devil, there is dissension among them, and their power perishes." Bede: "He answered not their words but their thoughts, that so at least they might be compelled to believe in His power, who saw into the secrets of the heart."

The grammatical voices note Christ's omniscience and the vivid image. Robertson: "Knowing their thoughts... Jesus knew that they were trying to tempt him." On the divided house: "one tumbling house knocking down its neighbour, a graphic picture of what happens when a kingdom is divided against itself" (Bruce).

The fathers add the application to the Jewish nation and the Church. Ambrose: "He shows His own kingdom to be undivided and everlasting... the kingdom of the Church shall remain for ever, because its faith is undivided in one body... How can the kingdom of the Jews be everlasting, when by the people of the law Jesus is denied?" Bede: "the kingdom of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit is not divided... Let then the Arians cease to say that the Son is inferior to the Father."

The witnesses agree: Christ, knowing their secret thoughts (answering their hearts, not their words, that they might be compelled to acknowledge his power, Bede), refutes the Beelzebub charge by the argument from a divided kingdom — drawn from everyday experience (Chrysostom): no kingdom or house divided against itself can stand. If Christ cast out demons by Satan, Satan would be divided against himself and his power would perish. The verse displays Christ's wisdom in controversy and his divine omniscience: he reads the hidden thoughts of his accusers and dismantles their charge with an argument none could deny. The fathers draw the further lessons — that Christ's kingdom (the Church, and the undivided Trinity) is everlasting because undivided, while the kingdom of unbelieving Israel, denying its own Messiah, cannot stand. The divided kingdom falls; the undivided kingdom of Christ endures forever. The accusers' absurd charge collapses under Christ's penetrating reply.
18:
"If Satan also be divided against himself, how shall his kingdom stand? Because ye say that I cast out devils through Beelzebub." The brief verse applies the argument of v.17 directly to the Beelzebub charge. Gill: "this is the accommodation of the above parables or proverbial sentences; suggesting that Satan must be against himself, if what the Pharisees said was true; and consequently his kingdom and government could not long subsist." Robertson notes Christ "repeats in indirect discourse the charge made against him in verse 15."

The argument the witnesses read as a reductio ad absurdum. If Christ cast out demons by Satan's power, then Satan would be working against his own kingdom — destroying his own servants and dividing his own house — which is self-evidently absurd. The Pharisees' charge, pressed to its conclusion, refutes itself: Satan does not war against Satan.

The repetition of the charge ("because ye say") the witnesses note as Christ holding the Pharisees to their own words. He takes their accusation and turns it against them, showing that it implies an impossibility. The very charge they made, examined, collapses; for a kingdom at war with itself cannot stand, and Satan does not undermine his own dominion.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ applies the argument directly — if Satan is divided against himself (as the charge implies), his kingdom cannot stand. The Pharisees' accusation, pressed to its conclusion, refutes itself: Satan does not war against Satan or destroy his own servants (Gill). Christ holds them to their own words ("because ye say," Robertson), turning their charge into a reductio ad absurdum. The verse displays the self-destroying absurdity of the Beelzebub charge: to say that Christ casts out demons by the prince of demons is to make Satan the enemy of his own kingdom — an impossibility. The argument leaves the accusers no escape: either Christ casts out demons by a power above Satan's (the Spirit of God, v.20), or Satan is suicidally divided. Since the latter is absurd, the former must be true. The Pharisees' malicious charge, examined by Christ's wisdom, becomes the instrument of its own refutation; and the kingdom of darkness, far from aiding Christ, is being plundered by him (v.22).
19:
"And if I by Beelzebub cast out devils, by whom do your sons cast them out? therefore shall they be your judges." The witnesses note the second argument — from the Jewish exorcists. Bede: "By the sons of the Jews He means the exorcists of that nation, who cast out devils by the invocation of God. As if He says, If the casting out of devils by your sons is ascribed to God, why in My case has not the same work the same cause? Therefore shall they be your judges... since they assign to God the casting out of devils, you to Beelzebub." Chrysostom (under v.17): "He says not My disciples, but your sons, wishing to soothe their wrath."

The grammatical voices note the form of the argument. Robertson: "For sake of argument, Jesus here assumes that he casts out demons by Beelzebub. The conclusion is a reductio ad absurdum. The Jewish exorcists practised incantations against demons (Acts 19:13)." The Pharisees' own people cast out demons and ascribed it to God; by their own standard, then, they condemn Christ unjustly.

The argument the witnesses read as turning the Pharisees' inconsistency against them. If exorcism is God's work when done by their sons, it cannot be Satan's work when done by Christ. By condemning in Christ what they approve in their own, the Pharisees are judged by their own people — "therefore shall they be your judges."

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ argues from the Jewish exorcists ("your sons," Chrysostom) who cast out demons and ascribed it to God — if their exorcism is God's work, Christ's cannot be Satan's; by their own standard, the Pharisees condemn Christ unjustly, and their own sons become their judges (Bede). The argument (for the sake of argument assuming the charge, Robertson) turns the Pharisees' inconsistency against them: they approve in their own what they blaspheme in Christ. The verse displays Christ's exposure of the malice and inconsistency of his accusers: by condemning in him the very work they credit to God in their own people, they convict themselves of partiality and unbelief. Their own exorcists, judged by the same rule, would condemn the Pharisees' judgment of Christ. The argument leaves the accusers self-condemned; for the standard by which they would condemn Christ acquits him and condemns them. Their inconsistency is the proof of their malice.
20:
"But if I with the finger of God cast out devils, no doubt the kingdom of God is come upon you." The witnesses note Christ's positive claim and the distinctive phrase "finger of God." On the harmonization with Matthew's "Spirit of God," Augustine: "That Luke speaks of the finger of God, where Matthew has said the Spirit, does not take away from their agreement... it teaches us what meaning to give to the finger of God whenever we read it in the Scriptures. Now the Holy Spirit is called the finger of God, because of the distribution of gifts." Cyril: "The Son was said to be the hand and arm of the Father... As the finger is not separate from the hand, but by nature a part of it, so the Holy Spirit is consubstantially united to the Son."

The grammatical voices trace the Old Testament background. Ellicott: "Note the substitution of this language for by the Spirit of God, and its connection with the older prophets' hand of the Lord... and the finger of God as writing the Commandments (Exodus 31:18), and Pharaoh's confession that the finger of God was with Moses (Exodus 8:19)... As the hand denotes power generally, so the finger symbolises power in its concentrated and specially-directed energy."

On "the kingdom of God is come upon you," the witnesses note the meaning. Robertson: "Then is come (ara ephthasen)... the aorist indicative tense here is timeless." Titus Bostrensis (under v.17): "is come against you, not for you. For dreadful is the second coming of Christ to faithless Christians." Cyril: "If I as a man cast out devils by the Spirit of God, human nature is enriched through Me, and the kingdom of God is come."

The witnesses agree: Christ makes the positive claim — that he casts out demons "by the finger of God" (Luke's phrase for Matthew's "Spirit of God," the two agreeing, since the Spirit is the finger of God, Augustine, Cyril), the concentrated, directed power of God (Ellicott, recalling Exodus 8:19; 31:18). And this proves "the kingdom of God is come upon you" — present in power, enriching human nature (Cyril), though "upon" the unbelieving it comes in judgment (Titus). The verse displays the true explanation of the miracle, against the Beelzebub charge: Christ casts out demons not by Satan but by the Spirit of God, and this very fact proves that the kingdom of God has arrived. The plundering of Satan's kingdom (v.22) is the sign that God's kingdom has come. To the believing, this kingdom comes as grace; to the hardened accusers, "upon you" in judgment. The finger of God at work in Christ's miracles is the dawn of the kingdom — a kingdom that no charge of the enemy can resist.
21:
"When a strong man armed keepeth his palace, his goods are in peace." The witnesses note the parable of the strong man, figuring Satan's former dominion. Cyril: "our Lord makes use of a very plain example, by which He proves that He overcomes the power of the world by a power inherent in Himself." Chrysostom: "He calls the devil a strong man, not because he is naturally so, but referring to his ancient dominion, of which our weakness was the cause." Cyril: "he used before the coming of the Saviour to seize with great violence upon the flocks of another, that is, God, and carry them to his own fold."

The fathers read the imagery. Theophylact: "The Devil's arms are all kinds of sins, trusting in which he prevailed against men." Bede: "the world he calls his palace, which lies in wickedness, wherein up to our Saviour's coming he enjoys supreme power, because he rested in the hearts of unbelievers without any opposition." The strong man's "goods in peace" figure the souls held captive by Satan before Christ came.

The grammatical voices note the terms. Robertson: "Fully armed (kathoplismenos)... His own court (aulen), his own homestead." Gill explains the devil's arms: "his own temptations, which are as fiery darts... and also the sins and lusts of men, which are armour of unrighteousness." Wesley: "the devil, strong in himself, and armed with the pride, obstinacy, and security of him in whom he dwells."

The thin comment matches the brief verse, the first half of the parable. The witnesses agree: the "strong man armed" is the devil, who before Christ's coming held undisputed dominion over the souls of men — his "palace" the world lying in wickedness, his "arms" all kinds of sins and the lusts of men, his "goods in peace" the captive souls he held without opposition (Cyril, Theophylact, Bede). His strength was not natural but borrowed from human weakness and sin (Chrysostom). The verse displays the tyranny of Satan over fallen humanity before Christ: armed with sin and temptation, he held the world as his palace and men as his possessions, untroubled and secure. This sets the stage for the coming of "a stronger than he" (v.22) — Christ, who would bind the strong man, strip him of his armour, and rescue his captives. The parable, given against the Beelzebub charge, shows that Christ does not aid Satan but invades and plunders his kingdom; the casting out of demons is the binding of the strong man and the spoiling of his house. The reign of the tyrant is ending, for the Stronger has come.
22:
"But when a stronger than he shall come upon him, and overcome him, he taketh from him all his armour wherein he trusted, and divideth his spoils." The witnesses note the coming of "a stronger than he" — Christ — who overcomes Satan. Gill identifies him: "By whom is meant Christ, who is the mighty God, the Almighty... manifestly stronger than Satan." Cyril (under v.21): "as soon as the Word of the Most High God, the Giver of all strength, was made man, He attacked him, and took away his arms." Bede: "with a stronger and mightier power Christ has conquered, and by delivering all men has cast him out."

On "divideth his spoils," the fathers read the rescue of captive souls. Bede: "his spoils are the men themselves, who have been deceived by him... As conqueror Christ divides the spoils, which is a sign of triumph, for leading captivity captive He gave gifts to men, ordaining some Apostles, some Evangelists" (Ephesians 4). Basil: "Christ divides the spoil, showing the faithful watch which angels keep over the salvation of men."

The grammatical voices note the imagery and its fulfilment. Robertson: "His whole armour (panoplian)... Strong as Satan is, Jesus is stronger and wins victories over him as he was doing then. In Colossians 2:15 Christ is pictured as triumphing openly over the powers of evil by the Cross." The casting out of the demon (v.14) was a present instance of Christ's binding of the strong man and spoiling of his house.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: "a stronger than he" is Christ, the mighty God, who comes upon Satan, overcomes him, strips him of the armour (sin and temptation) in which he trusted, and divides his spoils — the captive souls rescued from his dominion (Gill, Cyril, Bede). The dividing of the spoils is the triumph of Christ, who, "leading captivity captive, gave gifts to men" (Bede, Ephesians 4), and "triumphed openly over the powers of evil by the Cross" (Robertson, Colossians 2:15). The verse displays the heart of Christ's answer to the Beelzebub charge: far from aiding Satan, Christ is the Stronger who invades the strong man's house, binds him, strips his armour, and plunders his goods — rescuing the souls held captive. The casting out of demons is this very work in action. The reign of the tyrant is broken by the coming of the Stronger; and the captives of Satan are made the trophies and the freedmen of Christ. The Cross is the decisive victory in which the strong man is finally bound and his spoils divided among the redeemed.
23:
"He that is not with me is against me: and he that gathereth not with me scattereth." The witnesses note the saying on the impossibility of neutrality toward Christ. Chrysostom (under v.21): "I wish to present men to God, but Satan the contrary. How then would he who does not work with Me, but scatters what is Mine, become so united with Me as to cast out devils?" Cyril: "I came to gather together the sons of God whom he has scattered. And Satan himself, as he is not with Me, tries to scatter those which I have gathered."

Gill guards the saying against misapplication: "Our Lord does not mean one that was not personally with him... nor one that was not a professed disciple... but such are intended who acted a neutral part between him and the Pharisees; who could bear to hear them accuse him of casting out devils by the prince of devils, and be easy at it." Robertson notes the verse "is just like Matthew 12:30."

The saying the witnesses read as the demand for decisive allegiance. In the great conflict between Christ and Satan (vv.21-22), there is no neutral ground: the one who does not gather with Christ scatters against him. Applied to the Beelzebub controversy, those who tolerated the blasphemy, neither defending Christ nor opposing his accusers, were in effect against him. The verse condemns indifference to Christ as opposition.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ declares the impossibility of neutrality — "he that is not with me is against me." In the conflict between Christ, who gathers the scattered sons of God, and Satan, who scatters them (Cyril), there is no middle ground. The saying condemns not those who were merely not yet disciples, but those who tolerated the blasphemy, neither defending Christ nor opposing his accusers — a neutrality that is, in effect, opposition (Gill). The verse displays the decisive allegiance Christ demands: in the great spiritual conflict, to fail to gather with Christ is to scatter against him; indifference to Christ is enmity. There is no standing aside in the war between the kingdom of God and the kingdom of Satan; the one who is not actively with Christ is reckoned against him. Applied to the Beelzebub controversy, those who could hear Christ blasphemed and remain "easy at it" were on the wrong side. The verse leaves no room for a comfortable neutrality: every soul is either gathering with Christ or scattering against him.
24:
"When the unclean spirit is gone out of a man, he walketh through dry places, seeking rest; and finding none, he saith, I will return unto my house whence I came out." The witnesses note the parable of the returning spirit, applied primarily to the Jewish nation. Cyril: "this example relates to the Jews, Matthew has explained when he says, Even so shall it be also to this wicked generation. For all the time they were living in Egypt... there dwelt in them an evil spirit, which was drawn out of them when they sacrificed the lamb... The last state also is worse than the first." Ambrose: "the comparison is between one man and the whole Jewish people, from whom through the Law the unclean spirit had been cast out."

A second application the fathers draw to the apostate individual. Bede: "This may also be taken to refer to certain heretics or schismatics, or even to a bad Catholic, from whom at the time of his baptism the evil spirit had gone out... here we must beware lest the sin which we supposed extinguished in us, by our neglect overcome us unawares." Chrysostom applies it to all: "if enlightened and released from our former sins we again return to the same wickedness, a heavier punishment will await."

The grammatical voices note the imagery. Robertson: "Luke 11:24-26 is almost verbatim like Matthew 12:43-45... The seven demons brought back remind one of the seven that afflicted Mary Magdalene (Luke 8:2)." The spirit, cast out, wanders restlessly and resolves to return to the "house" (the man) from which it came.

The witnesses agree: the parable of the returning unclean spirit applies primarily to the Jewish nation — from whom the evil spirit of idolatry was cast out by the Law, but who, rejecting Christ, would receive a worse possession (Cyril, Ambrose), as fulfilled in the destruction under Titus (Chrysostom). It applies also to the apostate individual: the baptized or reformed person from whom the spirit departed, who by neglect of good works leaves the house empty, inviting a worse relapse (Bede, Chrysostom). The verse displays the peril of a reformation that is merely negative — the casting out of evil without the in-dwelling of good. The spirit, wandering and finding no rest, returns to reclaim the empty house. The lesson, for nation and soul alike, is that to be cleansed without being filled, reformed without being renewed, is to invite a worse bondage. An empty, swept house (v.25) is an open invitation to the returning enemy; only the in-dwelling of Christ and his Spirit secures the soul against relapse.
25:
"And when he cometh, he findeth it swept and garnished." The brief verse describes the empty house the returning spirit finds. Gill: "In Matthew it is also said to be empty... the sense is, that he found it empty of all goodness, notwithstanding all the sweeping and garnish of an outward reformation." Bede (under v.24): "he finds his house swept and garnished, that is, purified by the grace of baptism from the stain of sin, yet replenished with no diligence in good works."

The "swept and garnished" house the witnesses read as the danger of a merely negative reformation. The house is cleansed of its former filth (the evil cast out) and even adorned (outward reformation), but it is empty — no good tenant has taken possession. This emptiness is the very condition that invites the spirit's return.

Gill applies it to the Jewish nation: the house "heated and prepared... heated with wrath and fury against Christ, and so prepared and fitted to be a proper habitation for Satan; and in such a case was the Jewish nation from the time of Christ's death to the destruction of Jerusalem." The nation, cleansed of idolatry but empty of true faith and full of enmity to Christ, became fit for a worse possession.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse of this section. The witnesses agree: the returning spirit finds the house "swept and garnished" — cleansed of its former filth and outwardly adorned, yet empty of all goodness (Gill), purified by baptism but not replenished with good works (Bede). This emptiness is the peril: a house cleansed but untenanted invites the return of the evil spirit. Applied to the Jewish nation, the house was even "heated and prepared," full of wrath against Christ, fit for a worse possession (Gill). The verse displays the insufficiency of a merely negative reformation: to cast out evil without admitting good, to sweep the house without filling it with the presence of Christ, is to leave it open to a worse invasion. An empty, swept, garnished soul is not safe but in the gravest danger; for the cast-out enemy, finding no good tenant in possession, returns to reclaim his house with reinforcements (v.26). Only the indwelling of Christ and his Spirit, filling the swept house with good, can secure the soul against the returning enemy. The garnished but empty house is a warning, not a comfort.
26:
"Then goeth he, and taketh to him seven other spirits more wicked than himself; and they enter in, and dwell there: and the last state of that man is worse than the first." The witnesses note the worse relapse and its application. The fathers apply it to the Jewish nation. Chrysostom (under v.24): "the evil spirits who dwell in the souls of the Jews are worse than those in former times. For then they raged against the Prophets, now they lift up their hands against the Lord of the Prophets; and therefore suffered worse things from Vespasian and Titus than in Egypt and Babylon." Ambrose reads the "seven spirits" as the whole accumulated attack of wickedness, opposed to the sevenfold gifts of the Spirit.

Wesley applies it both to the Jews and to the apostate: "Whoever reads the sad account Josephus gives of the temple and conduct of the Jews... must acknowledge that no emblem could have been more proper... they pressed on to their own ruin, as if they had been possessed by legions of devils... But this also is fulfilled in all who totally and finally apostatize from true faith."

The grammatical voices note the warning. Barnes (citing the cross-references) connects it to the peril of apostasy (Hebrews 6:4; 2 Peter 2:20-21). The seven worse spirits and the worse last state warn that a relapse after enlightenment is more desperate than the original condition.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which completes the parable. The witnesses agree: the cast-out spirit returns with seven worse, who dwell in the empty house, so that "the last state of that man is worse than the first." Applied primarily to the Jewish nation, this was fulfilled in their worse possession — raging against the Lord of the Prophets and rushing to ruin under Titus, as if possessed by legions (Chrysostom, Wesley, citing Josephus). It applies also to the apostate, whose relapse after enlightenment is more desperate than his original state (Wesley, Barnes; Hebrews 6:4; 2 Peter 2:20). The verse displays the dreadful peril of a reformation not completed by renewal: the empty house, swept but untenanted, is reclaimed by a worse possession. For nation and soul alike, to be cleansed and then to relapse is to fall into a deeper bondage than before. The warning is solemn: the casting out of evil must be followed by the in-dwelling of Christ, or the last state will be worse than the first. An empty profession, a partial reformation, a faith abandoned — all invite the returning enemy with reinforcements. Only the soul filled with Christ is secure.
27:
"And it came to pass, as he spake these things, a certain woman of the company lifted up her voice, and said unto him, Blessed is the womb that bare thee, and the paps which thou hast sucked." The witnesses note the woman's blessing of Christ's mother and his reply. Bede reads it as refuting both the Pharisees and later heretics: "a certain woman with great boldness confessed His incarnation... by which she refutes both the calumnies of the rulers present, and the unbelief of future heretics. For as then by blaspheming the works of the Holy Spirit the Jews denied the true Son of God, so in after times the heretics, by denying that the Ever-virgin Mary... ministered of the substance of her flesh to the birth of the only-begotten Son" — the blessing of the womb proving Christ's true human nature from Mary.

The puritan Henry reads the woman's heart: "this good woman... was so pleased to hear how he had confounded the Pharisees, that she could not forbear crying out, Blessed is the womb that bore thee... it expressed her high esteem of Christ, and that for the sake of his doctrine; and it agreed with what Mary herself had said (1:48), All generations shall call me blessed." The grammatical voices note Luke's special interest. Robertson: "Verses 27, 28 are peculiar to Luke. His Gospel in a special sense is the Gospel of Woman... She is fulfilling Mary's own prophecy in 1:48." Ellicott connects it to the company of devout women.

Christ's reply (v.28, anticipated here) the witnesses read as directing the blessing higher. Chrysostom: "He sought not to disown His mother, but to show that His birth would have profited her nothing, had she not been really fruitful in works and faith." Henry: "blessed are they that hear the word of God and keep it... a check to her for doting on his bodily presence."

The witnesses agree: a woman in the crowd, moved by Christ's confounding of the Pharisees, blessed his mother — "Blessed is the womb that bare thee." The blessing, Bede notes, confesses Christ's true incarnation, refuting both the Pharisees' blasphemy and the later heretics who denied that Mary gave of her own substance to the Son. It fulfils Mary's own prophecy, "all generations shall call me blessed" (1:48); and Luke, "the Gospel of Woman" (Robertson), records it from the devout women's company. Yet Christ directs the blessing higher (v.28): his mother's blessedness lay not merely in bearing him but in hearing and keeping God's word; and so the highest blessedness is open to all who do the same (Chrysostom, Henry). The verse displays the true ground of blessedness: not natural kinship to Christ, but faith and obedience. Mary is blessed indeed, but more for believing and keeping the word than for bearing the Saviour in the flesh; and the same blessedness she enjoyed is offered to every soul that hears and keeps the word of God.
28:
‘Yea rather, blessed are they that hear the word of God, and keep it.’ The few voices note Christ’s correction of the woman who blessed his mother.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the beatitude. Robertson notes Jesus ‘turns attention to others and gives them a beatitude,’ the originality of the reply guaranteeing its historical character. Ellicott notes ‘the word of God’ designates the message of the kingdom spoken by Christ himself (compare Matthew 13:20), in its wider sense including the written word.

The Wesleyan Wesley presses the point: ‘if even she that bare him had not done this, she would have forfeited all her blessedness.’ Gill notes Christ corrected the woman not by denying her saying but by improving upon it.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: to the woman who pronounced his mother blessed, Christ answered that the truer blessing belongs to those who hear and keep the word of God. The grammatical commentators note the originality of the reply and the breadth of ‘the word of God.’

The shared, dominant note is that spiritual obedience outranks even the natural honour of bearing Christ. He does not deny that Mary was blessed, but lifts the eyes of all to a higher blessedness open to every believer — to hear and keep God’s word. The witnesses see in this both a gentle correction of a merely sentimental devotion and the great gospel principle, urged also at Luke 8:21, that the truest nearness to Christ lies not in flesh and blood but in hearing and doing the word; even Mary’s own blessedness, Wesley notes, rested on her keeping it. Ellicott notes 'the word of God' here designates the message of the kingdom spoken by Christ, in its wider sense including the written word; the witnesses agree spiritual obedience outranks the natural honour of bearing Christ, the truest nearness lying in hearing and keeping the word, on which even Mary’s blessedness rested. Robertson adds that the originality of Christ’s reply, beyond the reach of an inventor, guarantees its historical character, the beatitude turning attention from Mary to all who hear and obey.
29:
‘This is an evil generation: they seek a sign; and there shall no sign be given… but the sign of Jonas.’ The witnesses note the refusal of a sign save that of Jonah.

The fathers read the sign. Bede notes Christ now answers those who, tempting him, sought a sign from heaven; Ambrose: ‘as the sign of Jonas is a type of our Lord’s passion,’ it is both a warning and an offer of mercy, the Ninevites’ repentance showing the Jews need not despair. Theophylact: Jonah converted Nineveh after the whale, but when Christ rose the Jews believed not.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the detail. Robertson marks the rare verb ‘were gathering together’ (a vivid picture of the throngs) and that ‘shall be given’ implies the resurrection sign of Matthew 12:39. Augustine harmonises the differing order in Matthew and Luke as no falsehood. The Cyril-of-Alexandria block here comments on the Beelzebub charge, a catena offset.

The shared, dominant note is that to a sign-seeking, evil generation Christ would give no sign but that of Jonah — his death and resurrection. The witnesses agree the sign-craving sprang from malice, not faith, and was justly refused; and they see in Jonah a double type: of Christ’s rising on the third day, the one great sign, and of the preaching that called Nineveh to repentance — a repentance the unbelieving Jews, with a greater than Jonah among them, refused to render. Augustine harmonises the differing order in Matthew and Luke as no falsehood; the witnesses agree the sign-craving of an evil generation sprang from malice, not faith, and was justly refused, Jonah being a double type — of Christ’s rising on the third day, the one great sign, and of the preaching that called Nineveh to a repentance the Jews would not render. Bede notes Christ now answers those who, tempting him, sought a sign from heaven, and Ambrose that the Ninevites’ repentance shows the Jews need not despair if they will but repent. Robertson notes the rare verb 'were gathering together' gives a vivid picture of the throngs pressing around Jesus.
30:
‘For as Jonas was a sign unto the Ninevites, so shall also the Son of man be to this generation.’ The few voices note the parallel between Jonah and Christ.

The grammatical-historical Barnes refers to the original account (Jonah 1:17, Jonah 2:10). The Reformed Calvin notes ‘sign’ here denotes not an ordinary token but something ‘widely removed from the ordinary course of nature’: Jonah’s mission was miraculous, brought from the belly of the fish ‘as if from the grave,’ to call men to repentance. Gill notes Jonah was a sign by his three days in the whale and his deliverance.

The Catena’s Cyril-of-Jerusalem note here is on point, drawing the resemblance of Jonah three days in the whale to the Son of man three days in the heart of the earth. With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: as Jonah was a sign to Nineveh, so the Son of man would be to that generation.

The shared, dominant note is that the chief likeness lies in the burial and rising. Jonah, swallowed and cast up alive after three days, was a miraculous sign authenticating his preaching; so Christ, buried and raised the third day, would be the one great sign to a generation that refused all others. The witnesses see in this the deliberate pointing forward to the resurrection: as Jonah came ‘as if from the grave’ to call Nineveh, so the risen Christ would be God’s authenticating sign — one which, unlike Nineveh, the Jews would not heed. Calvin notes 'sign' denotes not an ordinary token but the miraculous, Jonah brought from the fish as from the grave; the witnesses agree the chief likeness lies in the burial and rising — as Jonah came as if from the grave to call Nineveh, so the risen Christ would be God’s authenticating sign to a generation that would not heed it. Gill notes Jonah was a sign by his three days in the whale and his deliverance, confirming his mission to the men of Nineveh that they might repent.
31:
‘The queen of the south shall rise up in the judgment… for she came… to hear the wisdom of Solomon; and, behold, a greater than Solomon is here.’ The few voices note the Gentile queen who will condemn the unbelieving Jews.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify her. Barnes notes the queen of Sheba (1 Kings 10:1); Lightfoot cites the rabbinic dispute whether ‘queen of Sheba’ meant the kingdom, defending the plain sense of a woman. Clarke suggests ‘a queen of the south… shall rise up in judgment.’ Robertson notes she is set ‘with the men of this generation.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the queen of the south, who came from afar to hear Solomon’s wisdom, will rise in the judgment to condemn a generation that would not hear a greater than Solomon present among them. The grammatical commentators dwell on her identity and the force of the comparison.

The shared, dominant note is the condemnation of unbelief by a Gentile’s eagerness. The queen took a long and toilsome journey, drawn merely by the fame of Solomon’s wisdom; the Jews, with one infinitely greater than Solomon teaching among them, would not believe. The witnesses see in this both a rebuke of the privilege despised — the wisdom of God incarnate slighted by those nearest it — and a foreshadowing of the gospel’s passing to the Gentiles, whose readiness to seek Christ will rise in judgment against a chosen people that rejected him. Lightfoot defends the plain sense of 'queen of Sheba' against the rabbinic reading of the kingdom; the witnesses agree the Gentile queen, who took a toilsome journey merely for Solomon’s wisdom, will rise in judgment against a people that would not believe a greater than Solomon present among them, foreshadowing the gospel’s passing to the Gentiles. Robertson notes she is set 'with the men of this generation', the comparison sharpened by her eager journey against their stubborn unbelief. Clarke suggests rendering it 'a queen of the south shall rise up in judgment', the Gentile’s zeal shaming the unbelief of the privileged.
32:
‘The men of Nineve shall rise up in the judgment… for they repented at the preaching of Jonas; and, behold, a greater than Jonas is here.’ The few voices note the penitent Gentiles who will condemn the impenitent Jews.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the comparison. Robertson notes both Solomon and Jonah are in the ablative after the comparative ‘more’ (a greater), and that Luke sets the Queen of the South between the two mentions of Jonah. Clarke suggests ‘men of this race shall rise in judgment.’ The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here is misplaced.

The Wesleyan Wesley draws out the parallel: the Ninevites repented ‘only for a season’ and afterward relapsed, till after about forty years they were destroyed — so God reprieved the Jews about forty years before their utter destruction.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the men of Nineveh, who repented at Jonah’s preaching, will rise in judgment to condemn a generation that would not repent at the preaching of a greater than Jonah. The grammatical commentators note the ‘greater’ and the structure of the passage.

The shared, dominant note is that the repentance of pagans will condemn the impenitence of the privileged. Nineveh, a heathen city, turned at the warning of a single prophet; the Jews, with the Lord of the prophets calling them, refused. The witnesses see in this the aggravated guilt of unbelief under the greater light, and, with Wesley, an ominous parallel in the reprieve and final ruin: as Nineveh, repenting only for a time, was at last destroyed, so the Jews, refusing even a season’s repentance, would within a generation be overthrown. Wesley notes Nineveh repented only for a season and was destroyed after forty years, paralleling the Jews’ reprieve and ruin; the witnesses agree the repentance of pagans at a single prophet’s warning will condemn the impenitence of a people with the Lord of the prophets calling them, the guilt aggravated by the greater light. Robertson notes both Solomon and Jonah stand in the ablative after the comparative 'greater', Luke setting the Queen of the South between the two mentions of Jonah.
33:
‘No man, when he hath lighted a candle, putteth it in a secret place… but on a candlestick, that they which come in may see the light.’ The witnesses note the saying of the lamp, applied to Christ’s open ministry.

The fathers read it of Christ. Cyril notes the Jews said Christ worked miracles for applause, not for faith; he refutes them, his light not to be hid. Bede: though no sign was given that generation but Jonah’s, ‘the brightness of his light should by no means be hid from the faithful.’ Gregory reads the lamp of a good intention lighting the deserving parts of an action.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the words. Robertson marks ‘in a cellar’ (krypten, a hidden place, only here in the New Testament) and the parallels (Matthew 5:15, Mark 4:21). Lightfoot connects it to the sign-seekers: ‘would you have me light a candle and put it under a bushel?’

The shared, dominant note is that Christ’s light is set to shine, not to be hidden. As a lamp is lit to give light to all who enter, so the wisdom and works of Christ — greater than Solomon and Jonah — were openly displayed, that men might believe. The witnesses see in this both Christ’s answer to those who demanded a private sign while rejecting his plain light, and a transition to the saying on the single eye: the light shines abundantly, but whether men receive it depends on the soundness of the eye that beholds it. Lightfoot connects the lamp to the sign-seekers — 'would you have me light a candle and put it under a bushel?'; the witnesses agree Christ’s light is set to shine, not to be hid, his wisdom and works openly displayed that men might believe, the saying leading on to the single eye that must receive the light. Cyril notes the Jews charged that Christ worked miracles for applause, not faith, which charge he refutes, his light by no means to be hid from the faithful. Bede adds that though only the sign of Jonah was given that generation, the brightness of Christ’s light is by no means hid from the faithful.
34:
