‘The light of the body is the eye: therefore when thine eye is single, thy whole body also is full of light; but when thine eye is evil, thy body also is full of darkness.’ The witnesses note the eye as the lamp of the body, applied to the understanding and intention.

The fathers read it morally. Theophylact: as the bodily eye, sound or dark, governs the body, so the understanding governs the soul. Origen: the understanding ‘desires only singleness, containing no dissimulation or guile.’ Chrysostom: if we corrupt the understanding, which can let loose the passions, we do violence to the whole soul.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Barnes and Ellicott note ‘the light of the body is the eye’ (so Matthew 6:22), the understanding being the light of the soul and its guide; Ellicott notes the moral sense closer in Luke. Gill reads the eye as the understanding directing the faculties.

The shared, dominant note is that the soul’s eye — the understanding and intention — determines whether the whole inner man is full of light or darkness. A ‘single’ eye, sound and sincere, fills the soul with light; an ‘evil’ eye, corrupt and double, fills it with darkness. The witnesses see in this the abundant light of Christ’s ministry meeting the inner faculty that must receive it: where the understanding is sincere, the light of the gospel illumines the whole life; where it is corrupted by malice or covetousness, as in the cavilling Pharisees, even the light becomes darkness. Ellicott notes the moral sense is closer in Luke; the witnesses agree the soul’s eye — the understanding and intention — determines whether the inner man is full of light or darkness, the abundant light of Christ’s ministry meeting the inner faculty, which, if corrupted by malice as in the Pharisees, turns even the light to darkness. Origen notes the understanding from its beginning desires only singleness, containing no dissimulation or guile, and Chrysostom that to corrupt it is to do violence to the whole soul. Barnes notes the understanding is the light of the soul and its guide, as the eye is to the body.
35:
‘Take heed therefore that the light which is in thee be not darkness.’ The few voices note the searching call to self-examination.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Ellicott renders it ‘see to it whether the light that is in thee be darkness,’ a call to ‘self-scrutiny’ of one’s primary beliefs and intuitions of right and wrong, lest all be vitiated at its source; it implies a Light by which to judge our light. Robertson notes the idiom ‘whether not’ (me) in an indirect question, marking ‘a pitiful situation if the very light is darkness.’ Gill bids men attend to the gospel light and not neglect it.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: men are to take heed lest the very light within them prove to be darkness. The grammatical commentators stress the call to examine the inmost principle of judgment.

The shared, dominant note is the peril of a corrupted conscience. If the eye of the soul — the understanding meant to guide — is itself darkened, the whole life is misled at its very root. The witnesses see in this a solemn warning, pointed especially at the Pharisees, who, esteeming themselves the enlightened guides of others, had so darkened their understanding by malice and false tradition that their light was darkness. They press the abiding lesson: each must scrutinise his own first principles by the true Light, Christ, lest, supposing himself to see, he be in fact blind, and his profession of light only deepen his darkness. Robertson notes the idiom 'whether not' in the indirect question, a pitiful situation if the very light is darkness; the witnesses agree men must scrutinise their first principles by the true Light, lest, supposing themselves to see, they be blind — a warning pointed at the Pharisees, whose professed light was darkness. Ellicott notes the call implies a Light by which to judge our light, a Standard by which to test our standard, lest all be vitiated at its very source. Gill bids men attend to the gospel light shining in its ministration, and not neglect or despise it.
36:
‘If thy whole body therefore be full of light… the whole shall be full of light, as when the bright shining of a candle doth give thee light.’ The witnesses note the promise of a wholly illumined life.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound the seeming truism. Ellicott notes the ‘apparent truism’ is the most expressive utterance of a truth: if the whole life is illumined by the divine light, if the character is in its measure perfect as the Father is perfect, with no passion or prejudice darkening it, then it is wholly bright. Robertson notes ‘with its bright shining’ (te astrape), as by a flash of lightning the light is revealed. Lightfoot reads it of bringing a candid, benign mind.

The Wesleyan Clarke and Wesley draw the lesson: when the light of Christ dwells fully in the heart, it extends to every thought, word, and action, as a clear flame lights a whole room. Gill reads the soul full of gospel light through the Spirit’s illumination.

The shared, dominant note is that a soul wholly given to the light of Christ is filled with light throughout. The witnesses unfold the apparent tautology: the body — the life in all its workings — that is fully illumined, with no dark part, shines like a room lit by a bright lamp, the light reaching every faculty. They see in this the blessed contrast to the darkened eye of the Pharisees: where the understanding is single and the heart open to Christ, the whole man is enlightened, holy, and happy, the light of the gospel directing all his conduct. Lightfoot reads it of bringing a candid, benign mind; the witnesses agree a soul wholly given to the light of Christ is filled with light throughout, like a room lit by a bright lamp, the blessed contrast to the darkened eye of the Pharisees, where the single understanding and open heart enlighten the whole man. Ellicott notes the apparent truism is the most expressive utterance of a truth, the character wholly illumined being perfect in its measure, with passion and prejudice no longer there.
37:
‘A certain Pharisee besought him to dine with him: and he went in, and sat down to meat.’ The witnesses note the invitation that occasions the woes.

The fathers mark the setting. Bede notes Luke’s ‘as he spake these things’ shows he was interrupted by the Pharisee’s request; Augustine: the Pharisee thought within himself, yet uttered no word, but he heard who perceives the heart, and ‘spared him by reproof.’ Chrysostom: almsgiving ‘makes all things clean.’

The grammatical-historical commentators note the meal. Robertson and Clarke explain the verb ‘to dine’ (aristesē) means the morning or noon meal, not the evening supper. Barnes suspects the invitation was ‘with a malignant’ design, to draw Christ from the people. Henry notes Christ said privately at table much that he later said publicly in Matthew 23.

The shared, dominant note is that Christ accepted a Pharisee’s invitation to a meal, which became the occasion for a searching reproof. The witnesses see in his going in both his willingness to associate even with those who watched him and the consistency of his witness — what he would say publicly in the temple he would say privately at the table. They note the meal was the day-meal, and that the Pharisee’s hospitality, whatever its motive, gave Christ occasion to expose the hollowness of a religion careful for the outside while the heart was full of ravening. Augustine notes the Pharisee’s unspoken thought was heard by Christ, who spared him by reproof; the witnesses agree his accepting the invitation showed his willingness to associate even with those who watched him, and the consistency of his witness, what he said privately at table being what he would say publicly in the temple. Bede notes Luke’s 'as he spake these things' shows he was somewhat interrupted by the Pharisee’s request, and Chrysostom that almsgiving makes all things clean and is more excellent than fasting. Clarke notes the verb 'to dine' means the morning or noon meal, not the evening supper, as distinguished by the Greeks and Romans. Henry notes Christ said privately at table much that he afterwards said publicly in the temple, his witness in public and private being all of a piece.
38:
‘And when the Pharisee saw it, he marvelled that he had not first washed before dinner.’ The witnesses note the Pharisee’s surprise at Christ’s neglect of the ceremonial washing.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the custom. Lightfoot distinguishes the washing of the hands from the dipping, noting it was not the custom to wash the whole body before a meal but the hands only. Robertson and Ellicott note ‘washed’ is literally ‘baptized’ (immersed), the Pharisaic standard of ceremonial purity (Mark 7:3-4); Barnes explains the custom arose because they ate with their hands. Clarke refers to the earlier discussion (Mark 7:2).

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the Pharisee was astonished that Christ sat down to eat without the customary ceremonial washing. The grammatical commentators note the technical force of ‘washed’ and the Pharisaic insistence on such purifications.

The shared, dominant note is that Christ’s deliberate neglect of a man-made tradition exposed the Pharisee’s misplaced zeal. The washing in question was no command of God but a rabbinic addition, on which the Pharisees laid the weight of religion; and Christ, by omitting it, both refused to bind men’s consciences with such traditions and drew out the deeper lesson he is about to teach — that God looks not on the cleansing of the hands but of the heart. The witnesses see in the Pharisee’s marvelling the very error Christ will rebuke: a religion careful for the outward form while careless of inward purity. Lightfoot distinguishes the washing of the hands from the dipping, it being the custom to wash the hands only before a meal; the witnesses agree Christ’s deliberate neglect of a man-made tradition exposed the Pharisee’s misplaced zeal, drawing out the lesson that God looks not on the cleansing of the hands but of the heart. Robertson and Ellicott note 'washed' is literally 'baptized' (immersed), the Pharisaic standard of ceremonial purity (Mark 7:3-4), the custom arising because they ate with their hands. Lightfoot notes it was not the custom to wash the whole body before a meal, but the hands only, this washing being a tradition of the elders.
39:
‘Now do ye Pharisees make clean the outside of the cup and the platter; but your inward part is full of ravening and wickedness.’ The witnesses note the charge of inward corruption beneath outward purity.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Lightfoot stresses Christ speaks of the persons, not the vessels — ‘your inward part’ — for ‘he that made that which is without’ is God, not the cup’s maker. Robertson notes ‘ravening’ (harpages, plunder) and ‘wickedness’: they keep the external rules but their hearts are full of plunder (Matthew 23:25). Barnes notes the terrible charge applied even to the host.

The Reformed Calvin notes Christ glances at their hypocrisy, ‘having no desire of purity except before the eyes of men.’ The shared, dominant note is that the Pharisees, scrupulous for the cleansing of cup and dish, were inwardly full of greed and wickedness.

The witnesses see in this the heart of Christ’s indictment: a religion that polishes the outside while the inside is foul. The cup clean without but filthy within is the image of men who keep the ceremonial law yet live by extortion and harbour wickedness. They note the boldness of the reproof — spoken at the Pharisee’s own table — and the abiding lesson, that God values not the outward show of purity but the cleansing of the heart, and that hypocrisy, careful only for men’s eyes, leaves the inner man defiled before the God who searches it. Barnes notes the terrible charge applied even to the host at whose table he sat; the witnesses agree the cup clean without but foul within is the image of men who keep the ceremonial law yet live by extortion, the boldness of the reproof matching the abiding lesson that God values the cleansing of the heart, not the outward show. Calvin notes Christ glances at their hypocrisy, having no desire of purity except before the eyes of men, as if they had not to deal with God who searches the heart. Lightfoot stresses Christ speaks of the persons, not the vessels, 'he that made that which is without' being God, not the cup’s maker.
40:
‘Ye fools, did not he that made that which is without make that which is within also?’ The witnesses note Christ’s appeal to the one Maker of body and soul.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Barnes notes ‘fools’ denotes both want of wisdom and wickedness, the conduct a cloak for sin; God who made the body made also the soul, and the soul’s purity matters as much as the body’s. Ellicott notes the question implies a parabolic application — positive ceremonial law and the eternal moral law have the same Maker, one not to be neglected for the other. Lightfoot lists the rabbinic currency of ‘fools.’

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: Christ calls the Pharisees fools, for the God who made the outside made the inside too, and both must be clean. The grammatical commentators note the force of the argument from the one Creator.

The shared, dominant note is the folly of cleansing the outward while neglecting the inward, since both are God’s workmanship. He who formed the body that is washed formed also the soul that is defiled; and to attend to the one while ignoring the other is to mistake the very nature of the God they professed to serve. The witnesses see in the ‘ye fools’ a sharp exposure of a religion that, however learned, was at root unwise and wicked — for true purity, willed by the Creator of the heart, must reach within, the inward man being as much his concern as the outward. Lightfoot lists the rabbinic currency of 'fools'; the witnesses agree the folly is to cleanse the outward while neglecting the inward, since both are God’s workmanship — he who made the body that is washed made the soul that is defiled, and true purity, willed by the Creator of the heart, must reach within. Ellicott notes the question implies a parabolic application — ceremonial law and the eternal moral law have the same Maker, one not to be neglected for the other. Barnes notes 'fools' denotes both want of wisdom and wickedness, the conduct a cloak for sin.
41:
‘But rather give alms of such things as ye have; and, behold, all things are clean unto you.’ The witnesses note the call from ceremony to charity, and debate the difficult phrase.

The grammatical-historical commentators canvass the crux. Robertson reads ‘the things within’ (ta enonta, the things in the dishes) — give as alms with inward righteousness and the outward becomes clean — ‘good Christianity, not Ebionism.’ Lightfoot takes it ironically, against the Pharisees’ overrating of alms. Barnes reads ‘such things as ye have,’ even if unjustly gained, given now in repentance. Clarke: ‘what is within… or at hand.’

The Reformed Calvin holds Christ withdraws the Pharisees ‘from ceremonies to charity,’ declaring it is not water but liberality that cleanses; not disparaging God’s ceremonies but rebuking those who think God amused by mere signs. Wesley reads it as acts directly contrary to rapine.

The shared, dominant note is that, against the cup washed outside, Christ commends the cleansing power of love expressed in alms. However the difficult phrase be rendered — the things in the dishes, what ye have, what is at hand — the witnesses agree the sense is that mercy and a giving heart, not ceremonial washing, make a man truly clean. They see in this a sweeping principle: what defiles is the selfish, ravening heart; what purifies is the love that, ceasing from rapine, gives to the poor — so that inward charity, not outward rite, is the true cleansing. Wesley reads it as acts directly contrary to rapine, showing the heart cleansed; the witnesses agree the sense, however the difficult phrase be rendered, is that mercy and a giving heart, not ceremonial washing, make a man clean — what defiles is the ravening heart, what purifies is the love that, ceasing from rapine, gives to the poor. Calvin holds Christ withdraws the Pharisees from ceremonies to charity, declaring it is not water but liberality that cleanses, not disparaging God’s ceremonies but rebuking those who think God amused by mere signs. Barnes reads 'such things as ye have', even if unjustly gained, given now in repentance as a token of a changed heart.
42:
‘Woe unto you, Pharisees! for ye tithe mint and rue and all manner of herbs, and pass over judgment and the love of God.’ The witnesses note the first woe — punctilious tithing while neglecting the weightier matters.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the contrast. Robertson notes ‘tithe’ (apodekatoute, take a tenth off) of rue and every herb, where Matthew 23:23 has anise and cummin, and that Luke has ‘the love of God’ for Matthew’s ‘mercy and faith’; ‘ought’ (edei) — these they ought to have done without leaving the others. Barnes describes rue as a small garden herb.

The Catena’s Cyril-of-Alexandria block here comments on the cleansing of the cup (a catena offset), and the Cyril-of-Jerusalem note on the Spirit is tangential. With so brief a body of genuine comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the Pharisees scrupulously tithed the smallest herbs yet passed over justice and the love of God.

The shared, dominant note is the inversion of proportion in a hypocritical religion: exact in trifles, careless of the weightiest duties. The Pharisees counted out a tenth of mint and rue but neglected judgment and the love of God — the very heart of the law. The witnesses note that Christ does not condemn the tithing (‘these ye ought to have done’) but its divorce from righteousness and love; and they see in the woe a warning against the religion that is meticulous in small observances while empty of justice and the love of God, straining at gnats while swallowing the camel of neglected mercy. Barnes describes rue as a small garden herb; the witnesses agree the Pharisees, exact in tithing the smallest herbs yet careless of justice and the love of God, inverted the proportions of religion, Christ condemning not the tithing but its divorce from the weightier matters — straining at gnats while swallowing the camel of neglected mercy. Robertson notes Luke has 'the love of God' where Matthew 23:23 has 'mercy and faith', and 'ought' (edei) — these they ought to have done without leaving the others undone.
43:
‘Woe unto you, Pharisees! for ye love the uppermost seats in the synagogues, and greetings in the markets.’ The few brief voices note the second woe — the love of honour.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark it. Robertson notes ‘the chief seats in the synagogues’ (the semicircular bench facing the congregation), singular here and plural in Matthew 23:6, a marked Pharisaic trait (also Luke 20:46). Clarke notes each affected to be a ruler in the synagogue. Gill adds the uppermost rooms at feasts.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the Pharisees loved the chief seats and the public greetings that fed their pride. The grammatical commentators note this love of precedence as a marked characteristic of the sect.

The shared, dominant note is the woe against religious vanity — the craving for honour and pre-eminence. The Pharisees, professing devotion to God, in truth loved the best places and the deferential salutations of men. The witnesses see in this a religion turned to self-display, seeking the praise of men rather than of God; and they mark the contrast with the humility Christ requires, for the love of the chief seat and the public greeting betrays a heart that serves not God but its own glory — a vanity that, however cloaked in piety, is the very opposite of the lowliness of true devotion. Clarke notes each Pharisee affected to be a ruler in the synagogue; the witnesses agree the woe falls on religious vanity — the craving for the chief seats and public greetings — a religion turned to self-display, seeking the praise of men rather than of God, the very opposite of the lowliness of true devotion. Robertson notes 'the chief seats in the synagogues', the semicircular bench facing the congregation, is a marked Pharisaic trait, repeated at Luke 20:46. Gill adds the uppermost rooms at feasts to the chief seats in the synagogues, the marks of a craving for honour. Robertson notes the chief seat is singular here, plural in Matthew 23:6, the love of precedence a marked characteristic of the sect.
44:
‘Woe unto you… for ye are as graves which appear not, and the men that walk over them are not aware of them.’ The witnesses note the third woe — hidden defilement.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw the figure. Robertson notes the ‘tombs which appear not’ (adela, unseen) would give ceremonial defilement for seven days (Numbers 19:16), hence usually whitewashed as a warning; here men walk over them unawares. Clarke and Ellicott distinguish this from the ‘whited sepulchres’ of Matthew 23:27: here the graves are unmarked, the corruption unseen. The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note here is misplaced.

The Wesleyan Wesley notes Christ likely fixed his eyes on the scribes, the graves ‘overgrown with grass’ so that men stumble on them and are defiled unawares. With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses agree on the substance: the Pharisees are like hidden graves that defile those who unknowingly pass over them.

The shared, dominant note is the secret, contagious defilement of hypocrisy. Unlike Matthew’s whitewashed tombs, fair without and foul within, Luke’s figure is of unmarked graves: the corruption is wholly hidden, so that men are defiled without knowing it. The witnesses see in this the peril of an undetected hypocrisy that, under a fair show of piety, spreads its pollution to others who, taking the Pharisees for guides, are corrupted unawares — a danger the more deadly because unsuspected, the hidden grave defiling the unwary passer-by. Wesley notes Christ likely fixed his eyes on the scribes, the graves overgrown so men stumble on them unawares; the witnesses agree the figure, unlike Matthew’s whited sepulchres, is of unmarked graves whose corruption is wholly hidden, the secret, contagious defilement of a hypocrisy the more deadly for being unsuspected. Robertson notes the unseen tombs gave ceremonial defilement for seven days (Numbers 19:16), hence were whitewashed as a warning, yet here men walk over them unaware. Clarke and Ellicott distinguish this from the whited sepulchres of Matthew 23:27: here the graves are unmarked, the corruption unseen. Robertson notes these three woes cut to the quick and evidently stung the lawyers and Pharisees who heard them.
45:
‘Master, thus saying thou reproachest us also. And he said, Woe unto you also, ye lawyers!’ The witnesses note the lawyer’s complaint and the turning of the woes upon his class.

The fathers mark the conscience and the offence. Cyril: ‘a reproof which exalts the meek is hateful to the proud’; Bede: ‘in what a grievous state is that conscience which… thinks the word of God a reproach against itself.’ Theophylact distinguishes the lawyers (interpreters of the Law) from the Pharisees (a sect).

The grammatical-historical commentators note the man. Robertson notes ‘thou reproachest us also’ (hybrizeis, a positive insult), the lawyers being usually Pharisees; Barnes and Ellicott note the lawyers were learned in the law, feeling they ‘stand or fall with’ the scribes. The Cyril-of-Alexandria and Greek-Expositor blocks here digress on Zacharias and the key of knowledge.

The shared, dominant note is that the lawyer, stung by Christ’s words, complains of insult, and Christ, far from retracting, extends the woes to the lawyers. The witnesses see in the lawyer’s reaction the working of a guilty conscience — counting faithful reproof an affront — and in Christ’s reply the fearless faithfulness that would not soften the truth to spare the learned. They mark, with the fathers, the wretchedness of a heart that hears the word of God and feels only reproached, and the boldness of the Lord, who proceeds to give the lawyers three woes of their own. Theophylact distinguishes the lawyers, interpreters of the Law, from the Pharisees, a sect; the witnesses agree the lawyer’s complaint of insult betrays a guilty conscience that counts faithful reproof an affront, while Christ, far from retracting, extends the woes to the lawyers with fearless faithfulness, sparing not the learned. Cyril notes a reproof that exalts the meek is hateful to the proud, and Bede that grievous is the conscience which hears the word of God and thinks it a reproach against itself. Robertson notes 'thou reproachest us also' (hybrizeis) is a positive insult, the lawyers being usually Pharisees who felt the charge touched them. Bede notes that in such a state is the conscience which, hearing the punishment of the wicked, perceives its own condemnation.
46:
‘Woe unto you also, ye lawyers! for ye lade men with burdens grievous to be borne, and ye yourselves touch not the burdens with one of your fingers.’ The witnesses note the first woe against the lawyers — burdening others while sparing themselves.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the charge. Robertson notes ‘grievous to be borne’ (dysbastakta) and the fierce indictment of the lawyers’ ‘pettifogging interpretations’ in their oral teaching (so Matthew 23:4). Lightfoot reads the ‘burdens’ as the yokes of tradition the lawyers laid on men yet would not touch. Clarke: they insisted on traditions they themselves disregarded.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the lawyers loaded men with heavy burdens of tradition they would not lift a finger to ease. The grammatical commentators note the indictment of their oppressive, hypocritical teaching.

The shared, dominant note is the cruelty and hypocrisy of teachers who bind heavy burdens on others but bear none themselves. The lawyers multiplied the yokes of tradition, laying on the people a load grievous to be borne, while they themselves evaded it. The witnesses see in this the mark of a false ministry — rigorous in exacting from others what it will not practise, burdening the conscience with man-made rules rather than easing it with the grace of God; and they contrast it implicitly with the yoke of Christ, which is easy, and his burden, which is light. Lightfoot reads the burdens as the yokes of tradition the lawyers laid on men yet would not touch; the witnesses agree the woe falls on teachers who bind heavy burdens on others while bearing none themselves, the mark of a false ministry that exacts what it will not practise, contrasted with the easy yoke and light burden of Christ. Robertson notes the fierce indictment of the lawyers’ pettifogging oral interpretations (Matthew 23:4), Clarke that they insisted on traditions they themselves disregarded. Robertson notes 'grievous to be borne' (dysbastakta), a fierce indictment of the lawyers’ oppressive interpretations of the written law. Gill notes Christ was so far from retracting that he repeated and confirmed his charge, naming the lawyers particularly.
47:
‘Woe unto you! for ye build the sepulchres of the prophets, and your fathers killed them.’ The few brief voices note the second woe — honouring dead prophets while sharing their murderers’ spirit.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark it. Clarke notes ‘ye build’ means rebuild and beautify the tombs (so Matthew 23:29); Ellicott notes Luke omits the ‘sepulchres of the righteous.’ Gill notes the woe falls on lawyers, Pharisees, and the whole nation of that complexion.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the lawyers built and adorned the tombs of the prophets whom their fathers had slain. The grammatical commentators note the rebuilding and beautifying of the sepulchres and the parallel in Matthew.

The shared, dominant note is the hollowness of an honour to dead prophets joined to the living spirit of their persecutors. By building the prophets’ tombs the lawyers professed reverence for them, yet, as the next verse shows, they only proved themselves heirs of the fathers who killed them — and were about to crown the nation’s guilt by rejecting the prophets and apostles Christ would send, and Christ himself. The witnesses see in this woe the self-condemnation of a religion that venerates the martyrs of the past while persecuting the messengers of the present, honouring the dead it dares not obey and resisting the living it ought to receive. Ellicott notes Luke omits the 'sepulchres of the righteous'; the witnesses agree the building of the prophets’ tombs, far from clearing the lawyers, only proved them heirs of the fathers who killed them — a religion that venerates the martyrs of the past while persecuting the messengers of the present. Clarke notes 'ye build' means rebuild and beautify the tombs (Matthew 23:29), the lawyers professing reverence for the prophets their fathers slew. Gill notes the woe falls on lawyers, Pharisees, and the whole nation of that complexion, who built the tombs they should have heeded. Adam Clarke notes they are about to show, by their conduct toward Christ and his apostles, that they are as capable of murdering a prophet as their fathers were.
48:
‘Truly ye bear witness that ye allow the deeds of your fathers: for they indeed killed them, and ye build their sepulchres.’ The witnesses note the subtle proof of their complicity.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the argument. Robertson notes ‘consent’ (syneudokeite, to give full approval, as in Saul’s consent to Stephen’s death, Acts 8:1): outwardly they build tombs as if disapproving the murders, but in truth they oppose what the prophets taught, just as their fathers did. Ellicott notes ‘allow’ means approve (so Romans 1:32). Clarke: they show by their conduct toward Christ that they are not degenerated.

The Wesleyan Wesley sums it: pretending reverence for the ancient prophets while destroying those God sends now, they witness that they are ‘of the very same spirit’ as their fathers. The shared, dominant note is that the building of the tombs, far from clearing them, convicts them: it owns the fathers’ deeds and continues their spirit.

The witnesses see in this a just and searching argument. The lawyers thought their honour to the dead prophets distinguished them from the murderers; Christ shows it does the opposite — completing the fathers’ work, the sons stand as witnesses to and approvers of it, killing in their own day the messengers their fathers killed in theirs. They mark the deep truth that reverence for past saints is worthless, and even self-condemning, where it is joined to the persecution of present truth; the tomb-builders prove themselves the true successors of the prophet-slayers. Clarke notes they show by their conduct toward Christ that they are not degenerated from their fathers; the witnesses agree the tomb-building convicts rather than clears them, for in completing the fathers’ work the sons stand as witnesses to and approvers of it, reverence for past saints being worthless where joined to the persecution of present truth. Robertson notes 'consent' (syneudokeite) is to give full approval, the same used of Saul’s consent to Stephen’s death (Acts 8:1), outwardly building tombs yet inwardly opposing what the prophets taught. Wesley sums it: pretending reverence for the ancient prophets while destroying those God sends now, they witness they are of the very same spirit.
49:
‘Therefore also said the wisdom of God, I will send them prophets and apostles, and some of them they shall slay and persecute.’ The witnesses note the saying and debate the meaning of ‘the wisdom of God.’

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh it. Barnes and Clarke hold ‘the wisdom of God’ is the Saviour himself, called the wisdom of God (1 Corinthians 1:24), who in Matthew speaks in his own person, ‘I send you prophets’ (Matthew 23:34). Robertson, finding no book to quote, prefers ‘God in his wisdom said,’ Jesus speaking ‘with confident knowledge of the divine counsels.’ Lightfoot compares the rabbinic ‘the rule of judgment saith.’

The Wesleyan Wesley reads it of what God’s wisdom has said in many places of Scripture, sending prophets under the Old Testament and apostles under the New. The shared, dominant note is that God in his wisdom foretold the sending of prophets and apostles and their persecution.

The witnesses divide over whether ‘the wisdom of God’ names Christ himself or God speaking in wisdom, but agree on the substance: it was God’s own purpose to send messenger after messenger, and the settled response of that generation, like their fathers’, would be to slay and persecute them. They see in this both the patient mercy of God, who keeps sending prophets and apostles to a rebellious people, and the deepening guilt of a generation that would fill up the measure of its fathers by murdering the very messengers of grace — so bringing upon itself the requital the next verses announce. Lightfoot compares the rabbinic 'the rule of judgment saith'; the witnesses divide over whether 'the wisdom of God' names Christ himself or God speaking in wisdom, but agree it was God’s own purpose to send prophet after prophet and apostle after apostle, and the settled response of that generation, like their fathers’, would be to slay and persecute them. Wesley reads it of what God’s wisdom has said in many places of Scripture, sending prophets under the Old Testament and apostles under the New, some to be slain and persecuted.
50:
‘That the blood of all the prophets, which was shed from the foundation of the world, may be required of this generation.’ The witnesses note the gathering of guilt upon that generation.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Robertson notes ‘may be required’ (ekzetethē, required as a debt) and ‘shed from the foundation of the world’; Clarke and Ellicott note the differing phrase from Matthew’s ‘all the righteous blood’ (Matthew 23:35), and that the ‘that’ (hina) may mark result, not design. Gill identifies it with the righteous blood of Matthew. The Cyril-of-Jerusalem note here is tangential.

The Wesleyan Wesley notes the blood ‘shall be visibly and terribly punished’ upon that generation. With so brief a body of genuine comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the accumulated blood of the prophets, shed through all history, would be required of that generation.

The shared, dominant note is that the long-gathering guilt of prophet-murder would fall, in requital, on the generation that crowned it by rejecting and slaying the Messiah and his messengers. The witnesses note that this was no arbitrary charge but the just consequence of filling up the fathers’ measure: by their persecution of Christ and the apostles, that generation made the whole stream of martyr-blood, from the world’s foundation, its own. They see the sentence fulfilled, with Wesley, in the terrible destruction that fell within forty years upon the nation, temple, and city. Wesley notes the blood would be visibly and terribly punished on that generation; the witnesses agree the accumulated guilt of prophet-murder, gathered through all history, would fall in requital on the generation that crowned it by rejecting the Messiah and his messengers, fulfilled in the nation’s ruin within forty years. Robertson notes 'may be required' (ekzetethē) means required as a debt, and Clarke that the 'that' (hina) may mark result rather than design, the blood shed from the foundation of the world. Ellicott notes the differing phrase from Matthew’s 'all the righteous blood' (Matthew 23:35), Luke saying 'the blood of all the prophets'.
51:
‘From the blood of Abel unto the blood of Zacharias… It shall be required of this generation.’ The witnesses note the span from the first to the last martyr and the question of which Zacharias.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the range. Robertson notes the blood of Abel is the first shed (Genesis 4:10) and that of Zacharias the last in the Old Testament canon, which ended with Chronicles (2 Chronicles 24:22) — a sweep from Genesis to Chronicles. Lightfoot and Ellicott note Luke omits ‘son of Barachias,’ which causes perplexity in Matthew 23:35, favouring the view that those words were a later addition; the Jews owned no Zacharias slain in the temple but Jehoiada’s son.

The Wesleyan Wesley notes it was fulfilled within forty years in the destruction of the temple, city, and nation. The Cyril-of-Jerusalem note here is tangential. With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses agree on the substance: from Abel to Zacharias, all the martyr-blood would be required of that generation.

The shared, dominant note is the comprehensive reckoning of innocent blood. From the first murder in Genesis to the last in the Hebrew canon, the whole history of prophet-slaying is summed up and charged against the generation that would slay the Lord himself. The witnesses note the canonical span — Abel to Zacharias being, in the Jewish ordering, the first and last martyrs of Scripture — and see in the omission of ‘son of Barachias’ a sign that the simpler reading is original; and they mark the dread fulfilment in the nation’s ruin a generation later. Wesley notes it was fulfilled within forty years in the destruction of the temple, city, and nation; the witnesses agree that from Abel, the first blood shed, to Zacharias, the last martyr of the Hebrew canon, the whole history of prophet-slaying is summed up and charged against the generation that would slay the Lord himself. Robertson notes Abel’s is the first blood shed (Genesis 4:10) and Zacharias’s the last in the Hebrew canon (2 Chronicles 24:22), a sweep from Genesis to Chronicles.
52:
‘Woe unto you, lawyers! for ye have taken away the key of knowledge: ye entered not in yourselves, and them that were entering in ye hindered.’ The witnesses note the gravest woe — the lawyers’ obscuring of the Scriptures.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound it. Robertson reads it as ‘a flat charge of obscurantism’: they refused to enter the house of knowledge themselves, locked the door, and hid the key from others (compare Matthew 23:13). Lightfoot notes they took the key to themselves, arrogating all learning yet teaching only ‘trifling stuff.’ Ellicott notes each scribe was admitted to office by the delivery of a key. Clarke: by their traditions they gave a wrong sense to the prophecies of the Messiah.

The Wesleyan Wesley reads it as obscuring the knowledge of the Messiah, the key of the kingdom. The Cyril-of-Alexandria block here is a catena offset. The shared, dominant note is that the lawyers, the appointed interpreters of Scripture, had taken away the key of knowledge — neither entering the kingdom themselves nor letting others enter.

The witnesses see in this the most damning charge of all: those whose office was to open the Scriptures had instead locked them, by false interpretation hiding the true sense that pointed to Christ. Few sins, Barnes notes, are so great as keeping the people in ignorance; and the lawyers, by their traditions, both barred themselves from the kingdom and hindered the seekers who would have entered. They mark the abiding warning against a teaching that, professing to expound the word of God, in fact obscures the way of salvation and keeps souls from Christ. Barnes notes few sins are so great as keeping the people in ignorance; the witnesses agree the lawyers, the appointed interpreters of Scripture, had taken away the key of knowledge by false interpretation, neither entering the kingdom themselves nor letting others enter — a warning against teaching that obscures the way of salvation and keeps souls from Christ. Lightfoot notes the lawyers took the key to themselves, arrogating all learning yet teaching only trifling stuff, and Ellicott that each scribe was admitted to office by the delivery of a key.
53:
‘And as he said these things… the scribes and the Pharisees began to urge him vehemently, and to provoke him to speak of many things.’ The witnesses note the rage the woes stirred up.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the fury. Robertson notes the better text ‘when he had gone forth from thence,’ the verb ‘to press upon’ (enechein, to have it in for one, used of Herodias’s hatred of John, Mark 6:19) and ‘to provoke him to speak’ (apostomatizein, to catechise, draw out impromptu answers). Clarke notes they questioned him on many points to entangle or irritate him. Barnes: they pressed many questions without time to answer.

The Reformed Calvin notes these latest reproofs are placed by Luke in connection with the dinner, though spoken near the end. The Cyril-of-Jerusalem note here is tangential. The shared, dominant note is that the woes provoked the scribes and Pharisees to furious hostility, pressing Christ with hostile questions.

The witnesses see in this the natural fruit of faithful reproof upon hardened hearts: not repentance but rage. Stung and unmasked, the scribes and lawyers set upon Christ, plying him with questions on many subjects, hoping in the flood to catch some hasty or incriminating word. They see in the rabbis’ vehemence — the same word used of Herodias’s deadly grudge — the beginning of the settled enmity that would seek his life, and a warning that the truth which should have humbled them only hardened them into plotting against the One who told it. Calvin notes Luke places these latest reproofs in connection with the dinner, though spoken near the end; the witnesses agree the woes provoked not repentance but rage, the stung and unmasked scribes and lawyers setting upon Christ with a flood of hostile questions, the beginning of the settled enmity that would seek his life. Robertson notes the verb 'to press upon' (enechein) is the same used of Herodias’s deadly grudge against John (Mark 6:19), and 'to provoke to speak' of drawing out impromptu answers. Clarke notes they questioned him on many points, hoping by the multitude and impertinence of their questions to puzzle or irritate him into a rash word.
54:
‘Laying wait for him, and seeking to catch something out of his mouth, that they might accuse him.’ The few voices note the snares laid for Christ.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw the figure from the chase. Robertson notes ‘laying wait’ (enedreuontes, to lie in ambush) and ‘to catch’ (thēreusai, to ensnare in hunting), ‘treating Jesus as if he were a beast of prey.’ Ellicott notes the better text, ‘laying wait to catch something out of his mouth,’ throwing light on the later tribute-question (Matthew 22:15). Barnes: they laid snares as men do to catch birds.

With so brief a body of comment the synthesis is short, but the witnesses are agreed on the substance: the scribes and Pharisees lay in wait, seeking to trap Christ in his words that they might accuse him. The grammatical commentators dwell on the imagery of the hunt — the rabbis stalking Jesus like prey.

The shared, dominant note is the deepening, deliberate malice of the rulers. No longer merely offended, they set ambushes, hunting for some word they could turn into a charge against him. The witnesses see in this the acting-out of a settled policy — the same that would shape the later trap about tribute to Caesar — and the close of the scene in confirmed enmity: the faithful woes, far from winning the lawyers and Pharisees, hardened them into plotters who lay in wait for the Lord’s life. They mark the solemn lesson that faithful reproof divides hearts, and that those who will not be humbled by the truth are often enraged into open hostility against it. Barnes notes they laid snares as men do to catch birds; the witnesses agree the rulers, no longer merely offended, set ambushes to trap Christ in his words, the acting-out of a settled policy that would shape the later tribute-question, and the close of the scene in confirmed enmity, faithful reproof hardening them into plotters against the Lord. Ellicott notes the better text, 'laying wait to catch something out of his mouth', throws light on the later tribute-question (Matthew 22:15), the acting-out of a pre-concerted policy.
